learning_outcomes.description: Describe the major theoretical frameworks used in archaeology and their philosophical foundations
Analyse how theoretical and methodological choices shape the questions archaeologists ask, the evidence they pursue, and the interpretations they can defensibly make
Assess arguments and interpretive positions, recognising the influence of reflexivity, positionality, and differing cultural values on the production of archaeological knowledge
Research, synthesise and communicate evidence and analysis effectively

assessments.due_date:
You will elect to give your presentation in-class in for one of these Weeks: 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11 and 12. Instructions for selecting your week will be provided in class and on Blackboard.

An electronic copy of your presentation is due in Turnitin by 8am (start of class) on the day of your presentation.
assessments.task_description:
From Week 4, in the first hour of class, individually or as a pair/group, you will give a presentation using Powerpoint to the class on a case study that illustrates a theoretical idea from the previous week's lecture. You will choose and research your own case study. It cannot be one of that week's set readings; you must find a different case that demonstrates one or more of the theoretical ideas from the previous lecture. You will need to select your week and theory topic by the Week 2 class.

Your Powerpoint slides must be submitted online by the start of the class you are presenting in. The presentations will be recorded as part of the standard Echo360 recording of the class. The presentations will be available on Blackboard for all students to see when they open the lecture recording.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The presentation is assessed in class, but you are required to also submit your presentation slides via Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
19/10/2026 4:00 pm

Engagement activities will be held in-class in Weeks 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11 and 12. You will need to make contributions in classes during at least five weeks of your choosing to create the portfolio content.
assessments.task_description:
Over the semester you help build the class's theory network map during seminars. To do this, you will make at least five in-class contributions over different weeks (two formal and at least three informal) and submit them together as an engagement portfolio. If you make more than five contributions during seminars, submit your best five for grading. The expected formats of contributions and examples are provided on Blackboard.

Your engagement portfolio will include:

  • Copies of all your two formal contributions
  • Descriptions of your three informal contributions and the week they were shared
  • A photo showing your contributions to the network map in context
  • For each contribution, include a statement of no more than 100 words explaining and reflecting on your engagement


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
14/09/2026 8:00 am

This exam will occur in the timetabled class for Week 8.
assessments.task_description:
For this exam, you'll be given an archaeological problem and, using the theories from class, will need to design a conceptual framework to address it, then reflect on and justify your work by responding to questions about the choices you made, potential interpretive outcomes and the links to evidence and methods. You should use the “theory mushroom framework”, as practiced in class, to articulate your conceptual framework.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
This task develops the conceptual framework you built in the Week 8 in-class exam into a refined, researched and defended piece, and locates it on the theory network the class built together. You will submit:

  • Refined framework: This is the considered version of what you drafted under exam conditions; you are free to incorporate what you'd change on reflection and with deeper research.
  • Network map: A visual representation of your framework and its relationship to the class network map.
  • Literature review structure: Present the architecture of a literature review chapter for the framework with annotations for each section.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.

learning_activities.topic:
Theory is your friend

Theory isn't a lineage of dead ancestors to memorise in order, it's a living network of approaches, authors and assumptions, connected underground long before they surface that can really help you 'do archaeology'. This week introduces the mycelial metaphor, names the threads that we'll follow all semester, and invites you to engage reflexively with theory in your own practice.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Theory is your friend Theory isn't a lineage of dead ancestors to memorise in order, it's a living network of approaches, authors and assumptions, connected underground long before they surface that can really help you 'do archaeology'. This week introduces the mycelial metaphor, names the threads that we'll follow all semester, and invites you to engage reflexively with theory in your own practice.

learning_activities.topic:
Setting the substrate: the foundations of archaeological theory

We begin digging into the foundations of archaeological theory, tracing the three early and influential trends that still shape the discipline: culture history, processualism and post-processualism.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: Setting the substrate: the foundations of archaeological theory
learning_activities.topic_description: Setting the substrate: the foundations of archaeological theory We begin digging into the foundations of archaeological theory, tracing the three early and influential trends that still shape the discipline: culture history, processualism and post-processualism.

learning_activities.topic:
Breaking dualities: theory in the new millennium

Moving past the processual/post-processual split, we unpack how the threads from Week 1 help us see the origins of archaeological theory differently. This week we'll begin a weekly 'mushroom framework' seminar section that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: Breaking dualities: theory in the new millennium
learning_activities.topic_description: Breaking dualities: theory in the new millennium Moving past the processual/post-processual split, we unpack how the threads from Week 1 help us see the origins of archaeological theory differently. This week we'll begin a weekly 'mushroom framework' seminar section that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Practice theory; agency and structure

This week we'll start to look beyond the processual/post-processual divide and unpack practice theory, agency and social structure. Student presentations (processualism; post-processualism; or middle range theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the 'mushroom framework' work that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Practice theory; agency and structure This week we'll start to look beyond the processual/post-processual divide and unpack practice theory, agency and social structure. Student presentations (processualism; post-processualism; or middle range theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the 'mushroom framework' work that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Identity, gender and personhood

This week we explore personhood, gender and identity as things made rather than given, drawing on feminist and queer theory to ask how archaeology has both obscured and recovered them. Student presentations (praxis; practice theory; agency) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Identity, gender and personhood This week we explore personhood, gender and identity as things made rather than given, drawing on feminist and queer theory to ask how archaeology has both obscured and recovered them. Student presentations (praxis; practice theory; agency) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section that will look at the practical applications of theory to research design, in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
The secret lives of things

Can personhood extend beyond people? We explore object agency and the biography of things, and where these ideas overlap with behavioural archaeology. Student presentations (queer theory; feminism; gender) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: The secret lives of things Can personhood extend beyond people? We explore object agency and the biography of things, and where these ideas overlap with behavioural archaeology. Student presentations (queer theory; feminism; gender) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Experiencing the past

We turn to the experiential past, exploring landscape phenomenology and materiality; how bodily, sensory engagement with place and things has been used (and contested) as a route into past lives. This week we'll also contrast this with other approaches to environmental interactions studied through cultural ecology and systems theory. Student presentations (Object biography; behavioral archaeology; object agency) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Student presentations (Object biography; behavioral archaeology; object agency) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
In class exam on Conceptual Framework Design

For this exam, you'll be given an archaeological problem and, using the theories from class, will need to design a conceptual framework to address it, then reflect on and justify the choices you made.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: In class exam on Conceptual Framework Design
learning_activities.topic_description: In class exam on Conceptual Framework Design For this exam, you'll be given an archaeological problem and, using the theories from class, will need to design a conceptual framework to address it, then reflect on and justify the choices you made.

learning_activities.topic:
Networks of things

We follow the mycelial metaphor to its theoretical home, tracing a line through contextual archaeology, Actor-Network theory and new materialisms, and asking what archaeology gains when humans stop being the only actors in the story. This is the week the metaphor turns reflexive: the course's own model is up for critique. Student presentations (phenomenology; systems theory; cultural ecology; or optimal foraging theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03

This is the week the metaphor turns reflexive: the course's own model is up for critique. Student presentations (phenomenology; systems theory; cultural ecology; or optimal foraging theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Mid-semester break

Not dead time! The network rests underground before it fruits...
learning_activities.topic_description: Mid-semester break Not dead time! The network rests underground before it fruits...

learning_activities.topic:
Study break (public holiday)

Not dead time! The network rests underground before it fruits...
learning_activities.topic_description: Study break (public holiday) Not dead time! The network rests underground before it fruits...

learning_activities.topic:
More-than-human: people, plants and animals

We frame the week through evolutionary theory, then open it into multispecies archaeology: archaeology's role in understanding the long, entangled relationships between people, plants and animals. Student presentations (new materialisms; contextual archaeology; actor network theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: More-than-human: people, plants and animals We frame the week through evolutionary theory, then open it into multispecies archaeology: archaeology's role in understanding the long, entangled relationships between people, plants and animals. Student presentations (new materialisms; contextual archaeology; actor network theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
Breaking from colonised things

We bring postcolonial theory, Indigenous knowledges and the ontological turn to the foreground, asking who gets to narrate the past, and what it means to take colonised things and other ways of knowing seriously. Student presentations (more-than-human; multispecies; evolutionary theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Breaking from colonised things We bring postcolonial theory, Indigenous knowledges and the ontological turn to the foreground, asking who gets to narrate the past, and what it means to take colonised things and other ways of knowing seriously. Student presentations (more-than-human; multispecies; evolutionary theory) will occur at the start of the seminar. At the end of the seminar, we will also continue the framework seminar section in preparation for the conceptual framework assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
New relations: a conclusion

We close by stepping back to map the mycelium that was there all along. Not "where we ended up on the tree" but the living network in all its messy forms, ready for your contributions.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03
